coredata.dc:title: Synergetic modification on buried and upper surfaces of perovskites with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for efficient and stable solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chao
Yao, Disheng
Dong, Pengpeng
Tang, Ziqi
Li, Ying
Chen, Bitao
Tian, Nan
Zheng, Guoyuan
Peng, Yong
Long, Fei
coredata.dc:description: Tin oxide (SnO2), which is used as an electron transport layer in perovskite solar cells (PSCs), however, has nonnegligible defects. The surface of SnO2 films contains hydroxyl groups (–OH), which increases film defects and affects the quality of the upper perovskite. The energy level mismatch is also found in SnO2, which leads to severe nonradiative recombination and unsatisfactory photovoltaic performance. Meanwhile, excessive PbI2 residues on the perovskite surface serve as catalytic decomposition sites, significantly undermining device stability. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by using human hair as a raw material and were employed as an additive for interface between SnO2 and perovskites to improve interfacial charge transport. In addition, these N-CQDs were also used as a surface modifier on top of the perovskite layer to reduce residual PbI2. These results significantly increase the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the related devices from 19.22% to 21.49%. The CQDs-modified device also shows significantly enhanced operational stability that when exposed to 30% humidity, the PCE of the optimized device retains 86% of its initial value after 1200 h. This study emphasizes the great potential of N-CQDs as a multifunctional interfacial modifier for fabrication of efficient and stable PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial Engineering
Carbon Quantum Dots
Operational Stability
Electronic Transport Additives

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: D.Y. thanks the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2022GXNSFBA035601), the Guangxi Research Foundation for Science and Technology Base and Talent Special (AD21220055), and Guilin Scientific Research and Technological Development Foundation (20220125-3). Guilin University of Technology Laboratory Open Foundation (RZ2300000636). F. Long thanks the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20245). Y. Peng thanks the National Natural Science Foundations of China (U20A20245 & U21A20171), and the Independent Innovation Project of the Hubei Longzhong Laboratory (2022ZZ-09).

D.Y. thanks the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2022GXNSFBA035601), the Guangxi Research Foundation for Science and Technology Base and Talent Special (AD21220055), and Guilin Scientific Research and Technological Development Foundation (20220125-3). Guilin University of Technology Laboratory Open Foundation (RZ2300000636). F. Long thanks the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20245). Y. Peng thanks the National Natural Science Foundations of China (U20A20245 & U21A20171), and the Independent Innovation Project of the Hubei Longzhong Laboratory (2022ZZ-09).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM and HRTEM Images of N-CQDs. b) Schematic diagram of N-CQDs (the blue sphere represents N-CQDs). c) Sn 3d X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/N-CQDs. d) FTIR information of N-CQDs and SnO2/N-CQDs.

A) XRD of perovskite before and after N-CQDs optimization. b) Pb 4f in the XPS spectrum with and without N-CQDs modification on perovskite. c) N 1 s spectrum. d) FTIR information of PbI2, N-CQDs, and PbI2/N-CQDs. e) Schematic diagram illustrating the structure of the PSCs and the mechanism of N-CQDs.

A) J-V curves under forward and reverse scans of the champion PSCs in the control and target devices. b) EQE spectra for the control and target devices. c) UV–Vis absorption spectra for the control and target devices. d) Conductivity curves for SnO2 and SnO2 (N-CQDs).

A) PL curves of control, SnO2@N-CQDs, PVK@N-CQDs, and Target. b) TR-PL curves of control, SnO2@N-CQDs, and Target. c) J-V curves of pure electron devices based on SnO2 and SnO2/N-CQDs. d) J-V curves of pure hole devices based on Spiro-OMeTAD and N-CQDs/Spiro-OMeTAD. e) Energy level diagram of PSCs. f) EIS spectra and equivalent circuit diagrams of control device and target device.

A) SCLC measurement of N-CQDs modified n-i-n PSC. b) SCLC measurement of N-CQDs modified p-i-p PSC. c) Dependence of VOC on light intensity for the control and target devices. d) J-V tests of the control and target devices under dark conditions.

T represents the percentage of PCE after device attenuation relative to the starting PCE. a) The performance test results of the unpacked control device and target device. Performance after storing 1200 h of storage in a constant temperature (25 ℃) and constant humidity (relative humidity, RH = 30 %) drying cabinet. b) Performance after 1000 h at room temperature with humidity above 80 %, showing normalized PCE. c) Photostability test of control and target devices in an environment with a temperature of 30 ℃ and humidity around 70 %. d) Champion J-V curves of the champion PSCs in the with and without N-CQDs devices. e) Champion J-V curves of the champion PSCs in the with and without N-CQDs devices. f) The thermal stability of unmodified carbon electrode devices without HTL and N-CQDs-modified devices are investigated by storing them at 85 °C in a N2 glovebox for 300 h, with the normalized PCE being observed.

head.ce:title: Synergetic modification on buried and upper surfaces of perovskites with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for efficient and stable solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optical and Electronic Material and Devices
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tin oxide (SnO2), which is used as an electron transport layer in perovskite solar cells (PSCs), however, has nonnegligible defects. The surface of SnO2 films contains hydroxyl groups (–OH), which increases film defects and affects the quality of the upper perovskite. The energy level mismatch is also found in SnO2, which leads to severe nonradiative recombination and unsatisfactory photovoltaic performance. Meanwhile, excessive PbI2 residues on the perovskite surface serve as catalytic decomposition sites, significantly undermining device stability. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by using human hair as a raw material and were employed as an additive for interface between SnO2 and perovskites to improve interfacial charge transport. In addition, these N-CQDs were also used as a surface modifier on top of the perovskite layer to reduce residual PbI2. These results significantly increase the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the related devices from 19.22% to 21.49%. The CQDs-modified device also shows significantly enhanced operational stability that when exposed to 30% humidity, the PCE of the optimized device retains 86% of its initial value after 1200 h. This study emphasizes the great potential of N-CQDs as a multifunctional interfacial modifier for fabrication of efficient and stable PSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have been developing rapidly in recent 15 years, but these devices still face many challenges, including severe non-radiative recombination and poor stability. Behind the problems mentioned above, bottom and top surfaces of a perovskite light-absorbing layer are found to play an important role in the influences of device performance, because these two interfaces impact charge extraction from perovskites to charge transport materials directly and primarily [1–7]. Tin oxide SnO2 films, as an electron transport layer (ETL) under perovskites in the n-i-p device structure, are usually prepared by spin-coating a water dispersion with SnO2 colloids, which results in the surface of SnO2 films often adsorbs many hydroxyl groups (–OH). These drawbacks trigger interface defects and energy level mismatch, which hinder development of photovoltaic performance of PSCs. On the other hand, on top of a perovskite layer, excessive PbI2 is unavoidable especially in the one-step spin-coating method for deposition of perovskite films due to nonstoichiometric reactant design for improving perovskite crystallinity. However, residual PbI2 on the surface serves as a catalytic site for degradation of perovskite materials, which weakens device stability [8].

In response to the issue mentioned above, interface engineering based on additives, with the advantage of operational simplicity, has been proven to be an effective strategy for assistance of charge carrier transport, improvement of energy level alignment, and removal of surface defects [9–20]. Among various agents, quantum dots (QDs) have become a helpful additive for interfacial modification of PSCs [21–30]. This is attributed to their outstanding charge transfer capability and modifiable molecular surface. Researchers have proposed various interface engineering strategies based on QDs. For instance, Mohamadkhani et al. introduced CdS nanoparticles between SnO2 and perovskite [31]. The CdS QDs were found to enhance the photoelectric current and reduce J-V hysteresis owing to suppression of interfacial charge recombination at the ETL. Zhao et al. employed amino-terminated ZnO nanocrystals to modify the SnO2 layer, which resulted in enhanced electron extraction and improved energy level alignment [30]. Additionally, the presence of amino groups provided coordination sites for Pb2+ on the perovskite surface. Despite ETL-based interfacial benefits, these metallic compounds are a rare sight in improvement of hole transport layer (HTL)-related interface due to their n-type semiconductor properties.

Herein, we adopt a green and cost-effective approach by using human hair as the raw material for synthesizing nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs). The N-CQDs are innovatively applied in both buried and upper interfaces surrounding the perovskite films, which is designed to improve the PCE and stability of PSCs. The positive effects of N-CQDs on charge carrier transport defect treatments are also systematically studied to demonstrate the working mechanism of N-CQDs. To broaden applications of CQDs, we fabricate N-CQDs-modified PSCs by replacement of spiro-OMeTAD with CuSCN as a HTL, and substitution of gold electrodes with doctor-bladed carbon paste. The successful applications of the N-CQDs in different types of devices provide new insights to interfacial engineering for achieving efficient and stable PSCs.

The hair samples were subjected to a sequential washing process with detergent, water, alcohol and acetone, followed by drying in an oven at 65 °C. The treated hair was then placed in a muffle furnace and pyrolyzed at 300 ℃ for 2 h. The product was finely ground into the powder. 1 g target powder was combined with 3 mL ammonia and 57 mL ultrapure water in a reactor. The mixture was kept in an oven at 150 ℃ for 24 h. The supernatant was then collected by centrifugation. Finally, the supernatant was subjected to suction filtration, dialysis and freeze-drying to obtain N-CQDs.

The Indium tin oxide (ITO) glass was ultrasonically cleaned with detergent, ultrapure water, acetone and alcohol in sequence for 15 mins and then dried in an oven at 65 °C. They were transferred to a 100-mW ozone-plasma treatment for 15 mins. Dispersion of 5 wt% SnO2 in water was spin-coated on the substrates at 4000 rpm for 25 s. The substrates were air-annealed on a hot table at 150 °C for 30 mins to obtain SnO2 thin film. Dispersion with N-CQDs was obtained by sonicating N-CQDs in deionized water. It was spin-coated on the SnO2 films at 3000 rpm for 30 s, then placed on a hot bench at 100 °C for 10 mins. The substrates were then placed in a 100-mW ozone-plasma treatment for 15 mins before being transferred to an N2 glove box immediately. The perovskite precursor solution was prepared by mixing lead iodide (PbI2), formamidinium iodide (FAI), methylammonium bromide (MABr) and Cesium iodide (CsI) in 1 mL mixed solvent containing Dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (4/1, v/v). The chemical formula of the perovskite was fixed to Cs0.05FA0.85MA0.10Pb(I0.97Br0.03)3, resulting in a precursor solution with a total molar quantity of 1.53 mol. To promote the growth of perovskite grains, excessive 5 % (molar ratio) PbI2 was added to the precursor solution. After being stirred at room temperature for 1 h, 5 wt% methylammonium chloride (MACl) was added and followed by one-hour stirring at 60 °C. 60 µL perovskite solution was spin-coated onto the SnO2-CQDs films at 1000 rpm for 10 s, followed by ramping up to 5000 rpm for 30 s. In the second step, 100 µL chlorobenzene (CB) was dripped onto the center of the film at the 25th second. The perovskite films were then annealed at 100 °C for 30 mins. Another dispersion with CQDs was prepared by ultrasonication of N-CQDs in CB. It was spin-coated on the surface of the perovskite film at 3000 rpm for 30 s. The samples were placed on a hot bench at 100 °C for 10 mins. The 2,2′,7,7′-Tetrakis [N,N-di (4-methoxyphenyl)amino]-9,9′-spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD) solution (72.3 mg Spiro-OMeTAD, 29 µL 4-tert-butylpyridine (4-TBP) and 17.5 µL lithium bis (trifluoromethanesulfonyl) imide (Li-TFSI) solution (520 mg mL−1 in acetonitrile) dissolved in 1 mL CB was spin-coated on the perovskite film at 3000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, 80 nm gold (Au) was thermally evaporated onto the Spiro-OMeTAD film under a pressure of 3 × 10-4 Pa.

Before discussion on the results, here is a statement of sample names. In this work, the PSCs takes four different device configurations. The main differences depend on the positions of CQDs. For ease of identification, they are named as follows: without CQDs (Control), SnO2/N-CQDs/Perovskite (SnO2@N-CQDs), SnO2/Perovskite/N-CQDs (PVK@ N-CQDs) and SnO2/N-CQDs/Perovskite/N-CQDs (Target).

N-CQDs, as a kind of zero-dimensional material, were synthesized by using human hair as a raw material through a series of reaction processes, including hydrothermal reactions. Fig. 1
a shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CQDs. The CQDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, which represents the (002) lattice plane of graphite. Meanwhile, the average particle size of the CQDs is 6.5 nm according to the statistic distribution diagram in Fig. S1 . It is also observed in the TEM images that the synthesized N-CQDs do not undergo serious agglomeration, which may be associated with functional groups linking to the surface of the CQDs. To further illustrate the composition information of the N-CQDs, XRD analysis was performed as shown in Fig. S2 . There is a wide diffraction peak at 24.9° (2θ degree), which corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of graphite[36]. This result is consistent with the TEM images mentioned above. Besides carbon, the N-CQDs contains functional groups such as amino and carboxyl groups, which is confirmed by the characteristic vibrational peaks of –NH2 and –COOH in the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) ( Fig. S3 ). In addition, Fig. 1 b shows a schematic diagram illustrating a simulated molecular structure of the N-CQDs.

Firstly, we studied the working mechanism of N-CQDs on SnO2. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on the SnO2 films with and without N-CQDs. Compared to the control SnO2 film, the XPS result (Fig. 1 c) shows that the signal peak of Sn 3d in the SnO2/N-CQDs sample shifts towards a higher binding energy. It is attributed to the esterification reaction between the –COOH in the N-CQDs and the –OH absorbed on the SnO2 surface [37]. As evidenced by the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1 d, a significant vibrational shift is observed in the peaks of -C = O of the –COOH. This suggests that an interaction has occurred between the N-CQDs and SnO2. In another word, SnO2/N-CQDs is not a simple physical contact. The FTIR result demonstrates that N-CQDs can be chemically anchored to the surface of SnO2 for an enhanced interfacial bonding.

Furthermore, the impact of N-CQDs on the transparency of SnO2 films was estimated by UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. The UV–Vis transmittance and absorption spectra in Fig. S4 and S5 illustrate that transparency of the SnO2 film with N-CQDs remains unchanged throughout the whole visible light range (300–800 nm). This indicates that introduction of N-CQDs does not alter the transparency of the SnO2 film to induce competitive light absorbing with the perovskite layer, due to quantum confinement effect. This also results in an optical bandgap (Eg) of SnO2 at around 3.60 eV shown in Fig. S6 .

To precisely study the influence of N-CQDs on wettability of the SnO2 films, dimethylformamide (DMF), as the main solvent for preparing the perovskite solution, was used to replace water for the contact angle test. As shown in Fig. S7 , the DMF contact angle of the N-CQDs-modified SnO2 film increases from 6.88° to 15.76°, which suggests an improved surface hydrophobicity. The increased contact angle contributes to reduction of SnO2 surface energy, which is helpful to accelerate nucleation rate of the post-deposited perovskite film. This is supported by the enlarged grain size of the perovskite film in the top-view and cross-sectional scanning electron microscope(SEM) images ( Fig. S8 )[38]. Meanwhile, Fig. S8 also depicts the corresponding grain size value distribution of the perovskite surface. The average grain size of the perovskite film increases from 400 nm to 600 nm owing to introduction of the N-CQDs.

The one-step deposition of the perovskite light-absorbing layer often requires an extra amount of PbI2 in the precursor solution for sufficient ion exchange and enhancement of the perovskite crystallinity. However, like a double-edge sword, the residual PbI2 forms Pb0-rich species that are regarded as catalytic sites for the material degradation. Besides the influences on the buried interface between SnO2 and perovskite, the N-CQDs are also found to be helpful to the upper surface of the perovskite film. To further study the effect of N-CQDs on perovskite and PbI2. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed, as shown in Fig. 2
a. The intensity of the peak at 12.1°, corresponding to the (001) lattice plane of PbI2, reduces after introduction of N-CQDs on the perovskite film. It indicates that the residual PbI2 is partially reduced by N-CQDs. We also performed SEM on the surfaces with and without the N-CQDs ( Fig. S9 ). There are significantly fewer bright small particles that belongs to PbI2 on the film with N-CQDs. The reduction of residual PbI2 is consistent with the XRD result mentioned above. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that the device efficiency and stability can be improved by modification with N-CQDs.

In addition, to analyze the working mechanism of the N-CQDs on the perovskite film, we conducted XPS analysis on the as-prepared and perovskite films with N-CQDs. According to Fig. 2 b and Fig. 2 c, both the XPS peaks of Pb 4f and N 1 s of the PVK@N-CQDs sample move towards lower binding energies, compared to the untreated film. The XPS result shows that the amino groups (–NH2) from N-CQDs can coordinate with Pb2+ from the residual PbI2 remained on the perovskite surface. This interaction can be further explained by the lone pair of electrons in amino groups (–NH2) of N-CQDs, which provides electrons for Pb2+ to be coordinated [39]. Also, in the FTIR spectrum results (Fig. 2 e), an obvious vibrational shift is observed at -N–H of the –NH2 stretching absorption peak. This agrees with the XPS results mentioned above. To visually summarize the mechanism of N-CQDs in PSCs, a detail scheme is exhibited in Fig. 2 d.

Given the improvements on microstructure of the SnO2 and perovskite layers by introduction of N-CQDs, the photovoltaic performance is then systematically investigated. The J-V curves for the target device and the control device are shown in Fig. 3
a. The results demonstrate that N-CQDs contribute to a significant increment of the PCE from 19.22 % to 21.49 %. This is mainly reflected by the enhanced JSC . This is due to the improved perovskite film quality and reduced carrier recombination at the interface. To check the protocol reproducibility related to N-CQDs, we fabricated 30 devices for each condition to obtain statistical J-V parameters of these PSCs. The statistic result ( Fig. S10 ) is highly consistent with the champion performance in Fig. 3 a. We also investigated influences of the N-CQDs concentrations on device performance. The data are summarized in Tables S1-3. Table S1 shows that the optimal concentration for achieving the champion efficiency is 0.8 mg/mL, when N-CQDs are only added into the buried interface under perovskite. In contrast, the optimal concentration is 0.5 mg/mL for the device with N-CQDs on top of the perovskite film in Table S2 . Table S3 shows the detailed photovoltaic parameters of the control and the target device. All the above results show that the optimal addition concentration of single-interface N-CQDs is consistent with that of double-sided modification. In addition, we measured external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra to quantify the increase of photocurrent. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the sample with the N-CQDs reveals a notable increase in the light absorption at the wavelength from 350 nm to 800 nm. This results in an increased integrated JSC of the PSCs, approaching 23.48 mA/cm2, which accords with the improved JSC in the J-V measurement mentioned above. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectra conducted on the perovskite films (Fig. 3 c) demonstrates that the N-CQDs-modified perovskite film has an enhanced light absorption intensity than that of the control sample. This may be the reason for the improvement of JSC . Fig. S11 shows that the N-CQDs has negligible effects on Eg of the perovskite film which keeps a constant value of 1.57 eV.

To investigate the electron transport properties of SnO2 modified with N-CQDs, a device structure of ITO/SnO2/Ag was constructed. Conductivity (
σ 0
) can be calculated by the following equation [40]:
(1)
J =
σ 0
A V
d

Here, A represents the effective area of the SnO2 film (0.062 cm2), and d represents the thickness of the SnO2 film (30 nm). Fig. 3 d shows the current–voltage curves of the devices. The conductivity was determined by the slope, which is proportional to the ratio of current to voltage. The conductivity of the SnO2 film modified with N-CQDs is 10.179
×
10-6 S·cm−1, higher than the conductivity of the control SnO2 film (5.283
×
10-6 S·cm−1). The data indicate an enhanced conductivity of the ETL after modification with N-CQDs, which is consistent with the increase of JSC in the target device.

Subsequently, the charge transfer and recombination dynamics were studied through the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) spectra [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the target film shows the lowest fluorescence intensity. The reduction of the PL intensity indicates an effective electron extraction and transfer of the target sample. Fig. 4 b shows the TR-PL spectra under a laser excitation wavelength of 485 nm. The peak intensity, which is indicative of the carrier transport lifetime, is calculated by using the double-exponential fitting equation below.
(2)
I
t
=
I 0
-
A 1
e x p ( +
A 2
e x p
( -
t
τ 2
)

Here,
τ 1
and
τ 2
represent the fast and slow decay components, respectively.
A 1
and
A 2
are attenuation amplitudes.
I 0
is a constant. The average carrier lifetime (
τ
ave
) is calculated by using the following equation:
(3)
τ
ave
=
A 1
×
τ
1
2
+
A 2
×
τ
2
2
A 1
×
τ 1
+
A 2
×
τ 2

The related calculation results are provided in Table S4 . Compared to the control sample (74.12 ns), the average carrier lifetime of the target device with N-CQDs decrease to 40.19 ns. The fast quenching of fluorescence indicates a rapid charge extraction and transport process in the target device. The N-CQDs-induced suppression of charge carrier recombination at the interfaces is then proved.

To further quantify charge extraction and transfer process, we analyzed the electron mobility and hole mobility of the related devices (Fig. 4 c and d). The electron or hole mobility (μ) is determined by using the space charge limited current (SCLC) model. The data are evaluated by the Mott-Gurney law formula below[43,44].
(4)
μ =
8 J
L
3
9 ε
ε 0
V
2

ε 0
represents the vacuum permittivity (8.85 × 10-12F·m−1),
ε
denotes the relative permittivity of the charge transport layer (CTL), L is the thickness of the CTL, J is the current density, and V is the applied voltage. The electron mobility of the control SnO2 is 1.94 × 10-5 cm2·V−1·s−1, while the electron mobility of SnO2/N-CQDs increases to 5.37 × 10-5 cm2·V−1·s−1. The increased electron mobility indicates an improvement of conductivity. On the other hand, the hole mobility of the control Spiro-OMeTAD film is 2.52 × 10-5 cm2·V−1·s -1. After modification with N-CQDs, the hole mobility of N-CQDs/Spiro-OMeTAD increases to 3.81 × 10-5 cm2·V−1·s -1. This suggests that the N-CQDs promotes charge carrier extraction and transport at both ETL and HTL.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), coupled with the related UV–Vis spectrum, was measured to determine the energy level of semiconductor materials. The tangents of the start and tail curve region determines the cut-off edge (Ecut-off) or the Fermi edge (Efermi) in the UPS. The Ecut-off, Efermi and Eg derived from the UV–Vis spectra are used to calculate the valence band maximum of (EVBM) and the conduction band minimum of (ECBM)[45,46].
(5)
-
E
VBM
=
E
H e
I
-
E
cut - o f f
-
E
fermi
(6)
E
CBM
=
E
VBM
+
E g

Fig. S12 and S13 show the Ecut-off and Efermi in the perovskite and SnO2 with and without N-CQDs. The calculated energy levels are illustrated in Fig. 4 e. The N-CQDs-modified SnO2 ECBM shifts up by 0.04 eV on one side, and the EVBM shifts up by 0.05 eV at the same time. The N-CQDs-modified perovskite ECBM shifts up by 0.12 eV on one side, and the EVBM shifts up by 0.12 eV at the same time. The adjustment of the energy levels makes the conduction bands of SnO2 and perovskite become closer. Similarly, the N-CQDs also leads to an improved valence band alignment between perovskite and Spiro-OMeTAD. The improved alignment of energy levels between the perovskite and the electron and hole transport layers facilitates improved interfacial charge transport.

To understand the mechanism of charge recombination in PSCs, we conducted electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS)[47]. Fig. 4 f illustrates the Nyquist diagrams and the related equivalent circuit. The Rs here is the series resistance of the device, and Rrec represents the recombination resistance. Detailed values of the fitted Rs and Rrec are given in Table S5 . The target PSC with N-CQDs has a higher Rrec than the control device. The increased Rrec implies that trap-assisted recombination has been passivated by interfacial modification with the N-CQDs.

To further investigate the effect of N-CQDs on defects of perovskite films, as shown in Fig. 5
a and b, we construct a n-i-n and a p-i-p device structures to detect its space-charge-limited-current (SCLC). To quantitatively assess the defect density in the perovskite films, three regions of the curve are identified. The ohmic region is at a low bias, where the current varies linearly with the electric field. The defect-filling-limited region is located at a moderate bias, where the current sharply increases. And the SCLC region at a high bias is not related to traps. The intersection of the two slopes in the ohmic and defect-filling-limited areas is used to determine the trap filling limit voltage (
V
TFL
). The trap density (
N
trap
) at the interfaces is calculated by
V
TFL
according to the equation below[48–51].
(7)
V
TFL
=
e
L
2
N
trap
2 ε
ε 0

ε₀ represents the vacuum permittivity. ε is the dielectric constant of the perovskite. L is the thickness of the perovskite films, and e is the elementary charge. The
V
TFL
values of the device prepared on control SnO2 and N-CQDs-modified SnO2 are 0.51 V and 0.40 V, respectively. The corresponding
N
trap
values are then determined to be 10.48 × 1015 cm−3 and 7.21 × 1015 cm−3, respectively. We also evaluated the hole mobility in the same way to estimate effects of the N-CQDs on the upper surface of the perovskite film (Fig. 5 b). The values of
V
TFL
with and without N-CQDs are 0.62 V and 0.54 V this time, which corresponds to
N
trap
of 16.02 × 1015 cm−3 and 9.72 × 1015 cm−3. The results indicate that the surface defect density of the perovskite film is significantly reduced by introduction of the N-CQDs.

To investigate influence of the N-CQDs on diode characteristics of the related PSCs, the dependence of VOC on light intensity was measured. We fitted the data by following the semi-logarithmic relationship between VOC and light intensity. The relationship between VOC and light intensity follows the equation below.
(8)
Voc =
m Φ
k b
T
q
l n
Φ
ph

m Φ
represents the ideality factor. k is the Boltzmann constant. T is the absolute temperature, and q is the elementary charge. When a radiative recombination occurs,
m Φ
shows a value close to 1. It correlates with the charge transport behavior. As shown in Fig. 5 c,
m Φ
in the device with N-CQDs is 1.16, while the control device has a
m Φ
value of 1.62. That is to way,
m Φ
significantly approaches 1 after being treated by the N-CQDs. The lower
m Φ
value indicates an effective suppression of nonradiative recombination in the target device. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S14 , based on the power law equation (
Jsc ∝
I
α
), the dependence of JSC on light intensity can be influenced by α, which represents the exponent factor. Compared to the control device (α = 0.9339), the device with the N-CQDs acquires a higher α value of 0.9482, which is consistent with the calculation of the ideality factor based on VOC .

To gain insight into the impact of N-CQDs on PSCs, we measured dark current density (
J D
) of the devices (Fig. 5 d). In comparison to
J D
of the control device, the
J D
of the target device decreases from 7.65ⅹ
10
- 4
mA·cm−2 to 2.68ⅹ
10
- 4
mA·cm−2. The significant reduction in dark current indicates lower current leakage and decreased recombination-induced energy loss caused by the N-CQDs[52].

We have conducted a thorough investigation on the stability of PSCs by estimation of their resistance to moisture, heat and continuous light illumination. As depicted in Fig. 6
a, the devices were monitored under a relative humidity (RH) of 30 % and at room temperature. Notably, the PCE of the target device remains 86 % of the initial PCE after 1200 h, while the control device exhibits a much more rapid decay with a retention of only 74 % in the same duration. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6 b, when exposed to the ambient air with a RH above 80 %, the target and control devices retained 70 % and 51 % of their initial PCE after 1000 h, respectively. This indicates that the stability of the target device exceeds that of the control device. To explore the reason, we conducted a contact angle test on the control sample and the target sample with water ( Fig. S15 ). Compared with the contact angle of the control sample (45.44°), the contact angle of the target sample increases to 61.35°. This indicates that the wettability of water on the target sample is weakened and the hydrophilic ability is reduced.

Furthermore, under light illumination with intensity of 1 sun (AM 1.5G), tracking the maximum power point (MPP) at the maximum voltage output the device, can obtain stabilized JSC and PCE (Fig. 6 c). The stabilized JSC of the device with N-CQDs is 21.53 mA/cm2, with a steady-state PCE of 19.38 %. In contrast, the related JSC and PCE of the control device are only 18.45 mA/cm2 and 15.99 %, respectively. This attributed to the reduction of perovskite photocatalytic sites by the reduction of shallow energy level defects in the target device.

In addition, to investigate whether introduction of the N-CQDs is a universal approach for enhancement of photovoltaic performance, we applied them in the various PSCs with different device structures. First, we applied it to the devices taking CuSCN as the HTL for replacement of Spiro-OMeTAD. The J-V curves of the related devices are shown in Fig. 6 d. It is clear that the PCE of the N-CQDs-CuSCN device is 18.16 %, which is higher than that of the device without N-CQDs (16.60 %). Fig. S16 shows the thermal stability test on the devices with CuSCN in the N2 atmosphere at 85 °C. The N-CQDs-modified sample remains 68 % of its initial efficiency after 130 h, while the PCE retention of the sample without N-CQDs is only 53 %.

Besides, we also applied N-CQDs to the PSCs with carbon electrodes instead of Au. It is noted that the active area of the doctor-bladed carbon paste is 0.7 cm2, which is much larger than that of the device with Au. The related J-V curves are shown in Fig. 6 e. The PCE (12.36 %) of the N-CQDs-modified device is significantly higher than that of the control device (10.28 %). Fig. 6 f demonstrates the PCE decline under the same thermal mentioned above. The N-CQDs-modified sample remains 76 % of its initial efficiency after 300 h, while the sample without N-CQDs holds only 47 %. To explore the reasons for the enhanced thermal stability, we conducted XRD tests on the control sample and the target sample, respectively. Place them on a hot stage at 85 °C for 5 h and then measure XRD to observe the changes. In the Fig. S17 , we express the crystalline quality by estimating the ratio of perovskite-(110) peak intensity to that of the PbI2-(001) lattice plane. We find that the ratio of the target sample before being heated is 14.02, which is higher than that of the control sample (8.31). It shows that the crystallization of the target sample is significantly improved by N-CQDs. After aging under thermal stress, the ratio of the target sample is 5.37, which is still better than the control sample (4.23). This provides a direct evidence for contributions of the N-CQDs on thermal stability of PSCs.

We have proposed a strategy to improve PSCs by using environmental-friendly and non-toxic N-CQDs as a modifier to treat both buried and upper surfaces of the perovskite films. The functions and working mechanism of the N-CQDs are figured out and summarized as follows.
1)
The introduction of N-CQDs on the SnO2 electron transport layer aligns the energy levels more effectively. That also expedites charge transport. N-CQDs enhances conductivity and electron mobility of the SnO2 film. This is responsible for the promotion of device photocurrent.
2)
The N-CQDs not only helps the perovskite film to obtain enlarged grain size, but also reduces surface defects induced by residual PbI2. This is beneficial to operational stability of the PSCs against moisture, heat and continuous light illumination.
3)
The positive effects of N-CQDs on interfaces can be extended to various device configurations. These configurations involve different charge transport layers and electrode materials, such as CuSCN and carbon paste. The introduction of N-CQDs has been proved to be a universal approach for achieving efficient and stable PSCs.

Chao Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Disheng Yao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Pengpeng Dong: Investigation. Ziqi Tang: Investigation. Ying Li: Investigation. Bitao Chen: Investigation. Nan Tian: Formal analysis. Guoyuan Zheng: Resources, Investigation. Yong Peng: Investigation. Fei Long: Supervision, Project administration.

Guilin University of Technology Laboratory Open Foundation (RZ2300000636). F. Long thanks the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20245). Y. Peng thanks the National Natural Science Foundations of China (U20A20245 & U21A20171), and the Independent Innovation Project of the Hubei Longzhong Laboratory (2022ZZ-09).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oriented crystallization of mixed-cation tin halides for highly efficient and stable Lead-free perovskite
Solar Cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cells, Improving the Efficiency and Stability of Perovskite Solar Cell through Tetrabutylammonium Hexafluorophosphate Post-Treatment Assisted Top Surface Defect Passivation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Collaborative passivation for dual charge transporting layers based on 4-(chloromethyl)
Benzonitrile Additive toward Efficient and Stable Inverted Perovskite Solar Cells
